elements.sent-2411-001: Zhaoye Zheng, Yurong Guo, Jiacun Wang,~\IEEEmembership{Senior Member,~IEEE, Jiyuan Yang, and Yangjie Xiao \markboth{Journal of \LaTeX\ Class Files,~Vol.~14, No.~8, August~2021 {Shell \MakeLowercase{et al. : A Sample Article Using IEEEtran.cls for IEEE Journals.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Transmission line defect recognition models have traditionally used general pre-trained weights as the initial basis for their training.
elements.sent-2411-003: These models often suffer weak generalization capability due to the lack of domain knowledge in the pre-training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this issue, we propose a two-stage transmission-line-oriented contrastive language-image pre-training (TL-CLIP) framework, which lays a more effective foundation for transmission line defect recognition.
elements.sent-2411-005: The pre-training process employs a novel power-specific multimodal algorithm assisted with two power-specific pre-training tasks for better modeling the power-related semantic knowledge contained in the inspection data.
elements.sent-2411-006: To fine-tune the pre-trained model, we develop a transfer learning strategy, namely fine-tuning with pre-training objective (FTP), to alleviate the overfitting problem caused by limited inspection data.
elements.sent-2411-007: Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the performance of transmission line defect recognition in both classification and detection tasks, indicating clear advantages over traditional pre-trained models in the scene of transmission line inspection.
elements.sent-2411-008: As a result, the traditional manual inspection methods no longer meet the growing demands for transmission line inspections.
elements.sent-2411-009: As a result, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) inspection technologies, supported by advances in UAV control systems~\cite{ref36, ref37 , have been widely adopted \cite{ref1, ref38 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Deep-learning-based computer vision (CV) models are employed to intelligently analyze transmission line images captured by UAVs, greatly enhancing the efficiency and quality of inspections~\cite{ref2, ref3 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In practical applications of the power industry, the visual foundation model (VFM) is generally used as the training starting point for these power-specific CV models to improve training efficiency~\cite{ref12,ref13,ref14 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The VFM is a fundamental model pre-trained on a large-scale dataset that can be adapted to various of downstream tasks~\cite{ref44 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=3.45in]{fig1} \caption{Training pipelines of different methods for developing transmission line defect recognition models.} \label{fig_1}
elements.sent-2411-013: Due to the domain gap between transmission line inspection images and general images, classical VFMs designed for the general domain tasks often perform poorly when directly used for power domain tasks ~\cite{ref4, ref5, ref6 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Various customized improvements have been proposed, including modifications to model architectures~\cite{ref8, ref9 and training strategies~\cite{ref7, ref11 for better capturing power-related semantic knowledge.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, none of these works improve the model from the initial training phase that has a crucial impact on the performance of the final model~\cite{ref45 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: They typically fine-tune the VFMs obtained from supervised pre-training on general datasets involving everyday objects, e.g., learning image classification on the ImageNet~\cite{ref43 , as shown in the yellow box in Fig.~\ref{fig_1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, such VFMs are not built up with power-specific semantics and lack the generalization capabilities necessary for effective defect recognition in transmission line inspections.
elements.sent-2411-018: Since contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP)~\cite{ref15 proved the feasibility of learning VFMs with strong transferability through natural language supervision, numerous works about VFMs based on vision-language pre-training (VLP) have emerged~\cite{ref39, ref16, ref17 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Pre-training on web-scale image-text data exposes the VFMs to extremely abundant concepts and enables them to acquire rich general semantic knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-020: Therefore, VLP-based VFMs can provide a starting point with stronger generalization capabilities for fine-tuning or zero-shot testing in downstream tasks than traditional VFMs based on supervised pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-021: More recently, a series of studies have incorporated industry-related knowledge into VLP algorithms to construct domain-specific VFM~\cite{ref18, ref19, ref20 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, no VLP-based VFM has been designed for the power domain.
elements.sent-2411-023: This motivates us to train a professional VFM for transmission line inspection via VLP, which contains abundant power-related semantic knowledge to provide a better training starting point for downstream defect recognition tasks.
elements.sent-2411-024: Due to the scarcity of the inspection data, training a VLP-based power-specific VFM from scratch is impractical.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, we train the power-specific VFM by adapting an off-the-shelf VLP-based VFM, i.e., Chinese CLIP (CN-CLIP) ~\cite{ref39 , to the power domain and following the traditional pre-training and fine-tuning pipeline commonly used in the power domain.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Here, we first tried a naive VLP-based method to integrate CN-CLIP and the VLP algorithm into the traditional pipeline by introducing additional textual training data and the corresponding pre-training task, namely image-text contrastive learning (ITC), as shown in the green box in Fig.~\ref{fig_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, we have noticed two key problems in the aforementioned VLP-based pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, in this paper, we focus on modeling power-related semantic knowledge hidden in the unique inter-class relations inside inspection images via VLP, as well as alleviating the overfitting problem in the fine-tuning stage with a dedicated strategy.
elements.sent-2411-029: Specifically, we propose the TL-CLIP framework which has a two-stage training pipeline for pre-training and transferring a power-specific VFM as shown in the red box in Fig.~\ref{fig_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The first stage involves power-specific VLP, where we introduce two novel pre-training tasks designed to model power-related semantic knowledge in potential inter-class relations.
elements.sent-2411-031: Among them, component type matching (CTM) helps to better discriminate the semantics of different types of components while defect-normality comparison (DNC) makes the VFM better understand the abstract concepts of defect and normality.
elements.sent-2411-032: The second stage involves fine-tuning with the pre-training objective where we introduce a novel transfer learning strategy named FTP.
elements.sent-2411-033: It incorporates an additional contrastive learning objective into the fine-tuning stage to alleviate the overfitting problem.
elements.sent-2411-034: With the proposed method, the downstream defect recognition accuracy is further improved and the overfitting problem is also suppressed, as indicated by the solid lines in Fig.~\ref{fig_2 (b).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=3.4in]{fig2} \caption{Two key problems in pre-training and fine-tuning a VLP-based power-specific VFM. (a) The existing pre-training task of the general VLP algorithm ignores the power-specific inter-class relations. (b) The fine-tuning stage of the pre-trained VFM suffers an exacerbated overfitting problem.} \label{fig_2}
elements.sent-2411-035: It is worth noting that our method improves performance in the power domain through dedicated power-specific training strategies without changing the original model structure, thus avoiding the increase in the number of parameters and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, our method is simpler and more efficient than traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.

elements.sent-2411-040: Section~\ref{sec3 describes how we pre-train our model and transfer it to downstream recognition tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section~\ref{sec4 demonstrates the experimental results to verify the effectiveness of our method.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally, Section~\ref{sec5 concludes this work and prospects our future work.}

elements.sent-2411-044: In order to bridge the gap between the general domain and the power domain, the latest research on transmission line defect recognition generally improves the general CV model from the perspective of model architecture and training strategy based on the characteristics of inspection images.
elements.sent-2411-045: Researchers often introduce more complex and expressive model architectures, such as attention mechanisms and feature fusion structures, to help the model capture the semantic knowledge contained in inspection images and model power-related semantic knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-046: Bao et al.~\cite{ref22 introduced the end-to-end coordinate attention module to YOLOv5, which enabled the model to focus on the features from key components rather than the complex backgrounds.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Guo et al.~\cite{ref23 designed a new feature enhancement mechanism used after the region of interest pooling layer of Faster R-CNN to obtain a more robust representation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Zhang et al.~\cite{ref24 augmented the DETR with a dilated encoder module to provide multi-scale features of transmission line components for better bolt defect detection.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Another line of works helps the model better learn the power-related semantic knowledge from the perspective of training data and objectives rather than model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-050: Li et al.~\cite{ref25 incorporated an intersection‐over‐union‐based sampling strategy into Faster R-CNN to alleviate the problem of class imbalance.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Zhao et al.~\cite{ref26 applied knowledge distillation algorithms to different types of object detectors to improve the detection accuracy on the limited dataset of hardware defects.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Zhang et al.~\cite{ref27 proposed a transmission line bolt defects generation network that generated abundant pseudo bolt images to improve the performance of bolt defect classification.}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, there is little research on exploring the appropriate training starting point, while most previous works ignore the importance of it.
elements.sent-2411-054: The most related work to this paper is~\cite{ref28 , the authors introduced self-supervised learning to pre-train an image encoder which then served as the backbone of the defect detector for fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Different from~\cite{ref28 which only leveraged vision modality, we explore the denser power-related semantic knowledge in image-text pairs via VLP to find a better and more professional training starting point.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Since CLIP achieved impressive zero-shot performance on ImageNet classification in 2021, a significant number of works in the general domain have been proposed for visual recognition tasks and vision-language tasks based on VLP-based VFMs~\cite{ref29, ref30, ref31 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In these works, VLP-based VFMs provide a better initialization with abundant semantic knowledge for subsequent fine-tuning or zero-shot testing.
elements.sent-2411-058: Motivated by the stronger transferability demonstrated by VLP-based VFMs compared with traditional VFMs based on supervised pre-training, researchers from different domains have tried to construct domain-specific VFMs based on VLP in the past few years.
elements.sent-2411-059: Wang et al.~\cite{ref32 proposed MedCLIP for medical image recognition which eliminates false negatives with medical knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Han et al.~\cite{ref33 designed two novel fashion-specific pre-training tasks and modified the model architecture for tasks of the fashion domain to pre-trained a model supporting multiple tasks of the fashion domain.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Mo et al.~\cite{ref34 introduced the idea of semi-supervised learning and designed two pseudo-labeling strategies to make the best use of unpaired data in specialized domains, which exceeds CLIP in both zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval tasks on the remote sensing benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In particular, Jeong et al.~\cite{ref35 first proposed WinCLIP for the domain of industrial manufacturing which supports both anomaly classification and segmentation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Unfortunately, the power domain still lacks such a dedicated VFM to support different types of defect recognition tasks in transmission line inspection.
elements.sent-2411-064: In this paper, we focus on the unique properties of transmission line inspection images and design a VLP algorithm for the scenario of transmission line inspection to train a power-specific VFM.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section, we first outline the two-stage training pipeline of TL-CLIP, with the first stage centered on a power-specific VLP and the second stage fine-tuning resulted model with the pre-training objective, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In the stage of power-specific VLP, our purpose is to pre-train a VFM that contains rich power-related semantic knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-067: Based on the inter-class relations of instances in inspection images, we propose two innovative power-specific pre-training tasks to address the limitations of image-text contrastive learning within the power domain, so that the model can better learn power-related semantic knowledge, as shown in the left side of Fig.~\ref{fig_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Specifically, we propose component type matching (CTM) to help the model better perceive the relation between different components through matching multimodal component instances, which avoids confusion between similar categories.
elements.sent-2411-069: Moreover, we further propose defect-normality comparison (DNC) which enables the model to better learn the abstract concepts about normality and defect by comparing normal and defective instances.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thanks to the power-specific VLP, the training starting point provided by our pre-trained VFM has significant advantages over the traditional ones and results in better defect recognition performance.
elements.sent-2411-071: In the stage of fine-tuning with the pre-training objective, we suppress the overfitting problem during fine-tuning for better downstream performance.
elements.sent-2411-072: We assume that the supervised learning objective in the traditional fine-tuning process will aggravate the overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-073: So we propose the FTP strategy which introduces the ITC task, i.e., the pre-training objective of contrastive learning, into the fine-tuning stage to alleviate this problem, as shown in the right side of Fig.~\ref{fig_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: While performing the ITC task, the image encoder of the pre-trained VFM also serves as the backbone of the downstream defect recognition model to learn the downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-075: In order to achieve efficient power-specific pre-training, we use the existing open-source VLP-based VFM for initialization to fully utilize the rich general knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-076: Here we employ the CN-CLIP ~\cite{ref39 as the baseline by default.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Based on the CN-CLIP pre-trained in the general domain, we focus on designing power-specific pre-training tasks so that the model can better learn the semantic knowledge specific to the power domain during pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-078: There are three pre-training tasks in our power-specific VLP, among which ITC is the basic task, CTM and DNC are the auxiliary tasks.
elements.sent-2411-079: Denote a batch of input images and their alt-texts as $\bm{I}$ and $\bm{T}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-080: Let: \begin{equation}\bm{V}=f_{i}(\bm{I}) \quad and \quad \bm{L}=f_{t}(\bm{T}) \, ,\end{equation} where $f_{i}\left ( \cdot \right )$ is the image encoder, $f_{t}\left ( \cdot \right )$ is the text encoder, $\bm{V =\left [\bm{v_1 , \bm{v_2 , \cdots, \bm{v_n \right]$ is the image feature matrix, $\bm{L =\left [ \bm{l_1 , \bm{l_2 , \cdots, \bm{l_n \right ]$ is the text feature matrix and $n$ is the batch size.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Then the contrastive learning loss can be calculated from two directions: \begin{equation}\label{itc_i2t} L_{itc}^{i \rightarrow t} =-\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\exp \left(\bm{v_{i}} \cdot \bm{l_{i}} / \tau \right)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp \left( \bm{v_{i}} \cdot \bm{l_{j}} / \tau \right)} \,,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{itc_t2i} L_{itc}^{t \rightarrow i} =-\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\exp \left( \bm{l_{i}} \cdot \bm{v_{i}} / \tau \right)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp \left( \bm{l_{i}} \cdot \bm{v_{j}} / \tau \right)} \,,\end{equation} where $\tau$ is the temperature hyper-parameter.
elements.sent-2411-082: Finally, the loss of the ITC task can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\label{itc} L_{itc} =\left( L_{itc}^{i \rightarrow t} + L_{itc}^{t \rightarrow i} \right) / 2 \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-083: Based on the above widespread relations in inspection images, we design the CTM task to help the model better distinguish different types of components and capture the similarities as well as differences between the same type of components.
elements.sent-2411-084: In the CTM task, the model is trained to match two randomly paired instances and judge the type of inter-class relation between them.
elements.sent-2411-085: To fully align the semantics between and within modalities, CTM includes four sub-tasks, namely image-text type matching (ITTM), text-image type matching (TITM), image-image type matching (IITM), and text-text type matching (TTTM).
elements.sent-2411-086: Their implementation is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_4 .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Taking ITTM as an example, we leverage the aforementioned image feature matrix $\bm{V}$ and text feature matrix $\bm{L}$ to implement the sub-task.
elements.sent-2411-088: First, we reorganize the batch of instances to facilitate learning relation judgment by shuffling the order of the feature vectors in $\bm{L}$ according to a random seed: \begin{equation}\bm{L^{\prime}} = \left[\bm{l^{\prime}_1}, \bm{l^{\prime}_2}, \cdots, \bm{l^{\prime}_n} \right],\end{equation} where $\bm{L^{\prime }$ is the text feature matrix with shuffled feature vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-089: As a result, the feature vectors at the same position in $\bm{V}$ and $\bm{L^{\prime }$ form a random instance pair.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Then we use the following formula to model the relations within the random instance pairs of the batch: \begin{equation}\bm{P^{ittm}} = Softmax \left(Concat\left( \bm{V}, \bm{L^{\prime}} \right) \cdot \bm{W_{ctm}} \right),\end{equation} where $Concat\left ( \cdot,\cdot \right )$ is a concatenation function that concatenates the corresponding feature vectors in $\bm{V}$ and $\bm{L^{\prime }$ to obtain a joint feature matrix of shape $n\times2d$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: On the other hand, to generate the ground truth matrix, we shuffle the original label vector $\bm{y =\left[y_1, y_2, \cdots, y_n \right]$ according to the same random seed used in $L^{\prime}$: \begin{equation}\bm{y^{\prime}} = \left[y^{\prime}_1, y^{\prime}_2, \cdots, y^{\prime}_n \right],\end{equation} where $\bm{y^{\prime }$ is the shuffled label vector and $y^{\prime _i$ is the label corresponding to $\bm{l^{\prime _i}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Next, we define function $\phi$ that judges the inter-class relation between each random instance pair in $\bm{y}$ and $\bm{y^{\prime }$: \begin{equation}\phi\left(a,b \right) = \begin{cases} 1 \text{if $<a,b>$ is of STSS} 2 \text{if $<a,b>$ is of STDS} 3 \text{if $<a,b>$ is of DT} \end{cases},\end{equation} where $<\cdot , \cdot>$ indicates the relation between instances $a$ and $b$.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\phi\left(a,b \right) =  1 if $<a,b>$ is of STSS 2 if $<a,b>$ is of STDS 3 if $<a,b>$ is of DT ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: With the definition of $\phi$, we can generate the corresponding label vector in the ground truth matrix $\bm{Y^{ittm }$ as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{y^{ittm}_i} = Onehot\left(\phi\left( y_i,y^{\prime}_i \right) \right) \,,\end{equation} where $Onehot\left ( \cdot \right )$ is the one-hot encoding function.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, the loss function of the ITTM sub-task can be formulated as: \begin{equation}L_{ittm} =-\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{3} y^{ittm}_{ij} \log p^{ittm}_{ij} \, ,\end{equation} where $y^{ittm _{ij}$ and $p^{ittm _{ij}$ represent the $j$-th element in $\bm{y^{ittm _i}$ and $\bm{p^{ittm _i}$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Similarly, we can also implement the TITM, IITM, and TTTM by reorganizing the instances of the batch in an inter-modal or intra-modal manner, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_4 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: The loss of the CTM task can be expressed as: \begin{equation}L_{ctm} =\left( L_{ittm} + L_{titm} + L_{iitm} + L_{tttm} \right) /4 \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-097: This means that the CTM task is still not sufficient to learn the concepts of normality and defect.
elements.sent-2411-098: By observing the data, we note that each component type with both defective and normal status can involve a pair or group of categories.
elements.sent-2411-099: For example, the component type of "grading ring" includes a pair of categories namely "normal grading ring" and "grading ring damage".
elements.sent-2411-100: Based on the observation, we further proposed the DNC task to make the model better understand the abstract concepts of normality and defect.
elements.sent-2411-101: In the DNC task, we train the model to compare the instances belonging to the same component type and find instances with the same state.
elements.sent-2411-102: We also divide the DNC task into multiple sub-tasks according to the component types with both defective and normal categories in the pre-training dataset, which can be found in Section~\ref{dnc_subtasks .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Taking the component type of grading ring as an example, we denote the corresponding sub-task as grading ring comparison (GRC), the implementation is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_5 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: First, we need to filter out the feature vectors corresponding to defective and normal grading rings from $\bm{V}$, respectively: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \bm{V^{dgr}} = \sigma\left( \bm{V}, \bm{y}, i_{dgr} \right)= \left [\bm{v^{dgr}_1}, \bm{v^{dgr}_2}, \cdots, \bm{v^{dgr}_k} \right], \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{split} \bm{V^{ngr}} = \sigma\left( \bm{V}, \bm{y}, i_{ngr} \right)= \left [\bm{v^{ngr}_1}, \bm{v^{ngr}_2}, \cdots, \bm{v^{ngr}_q} \right], \end{split}\end{equation} where $\sigma\left ( \cdot, \cdot, \cdot \right )$ is a filter function that filters out the feature vectors of the specified category from $\bm{V}$ according to the label vector $\bm{y}$ and the index of the category.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=3.4in]{fig5} \caption{The implementation of DNC sub-tasks, taking GRC as an example. For the sake of simplicity, we omit the image encoding and filtering process and only highlight the comparison between defective and normal instances.} \label{fig_5}
elements.sent-2411-105: Then we concatenate $\bm{v^{dgr }$ and $\bm{v^{ngr }$ to obtain a defect-normality feature matrix of grading rings: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \bm{V^{gr}} = Concat\left( \bm{V^{dgr}}, \bm{V^{ngr}} \right), \end{split}\end{equation} where $\bm{V^{gr }\in R^{m \times d}$ is the defect-normality feature matrix of all grading rings in the batch.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Finally, we calculate the cosine similarity between $\bm{V^{gr }$ and its transposed matrix to obtain the similarity score matrix: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \bm{S^{gr}} = Sigmoid\left( \bm{V^{gr}} \cdot \left(\bm{V^{gr}}\right)^{\top} \right) = \left( s^{gr}_{ij} \right)_{m\times m} \, , \end{split}\end{equation} where $\bm{S^{gr }$ is the similarity score matrix.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Meanwhile, a ground truth matrix for this sub-task is generated according to $k$ and $q$: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \bm{Z^{gr}}=\left( z^{gr}_{ij} \right)_{m\times m}, \end{split}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{split} z^{gr}_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 \text{$1\leq i,j \leq k$ or $k< i,j \leq k+q$} 0 \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{split},\end{equation} Then we calculate the loss function of the GRC sub-task as follows: \begin{equation}L_{grc} =-\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ z^{gr}_{ij} \log s^{gr}_{ij} + \left( 1- z^{gr}_{ij} \right) \log \left(1-s^{gr}_{ij} \right)\right],\end{equation} We can also perform sub-tasks corresponding to other component types with similar processes.
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation} z^{gr}_{ij} =  1 $1\leq i,j \leq k$ or $k< i,j \leq k+q$ 0 otherwise  ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-108: Finally, the loss of the DNC task can be formulated as \begin{equation}L_{dnc} =\frac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t}L_{dnc(i)} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-109: It is worth noting that the DNC task can be easily extended to other component types by adding only a few lines of code.
elements.sent-2411-110: When we use the aforementioned three pre-training tasks to pre-train the CN-CLIP, the total loss of the power-specific VLP stage can be formulated as \begin{equation}L_{pre-train} = \lambda_{1}L_{itc} + \lambda_{2}L_{ctm} + \lambda_{3}L_{dnc} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Stage 2: Fine-tuning with the Pre-training Objective
elements.sent-2411-111: Since the inspection data that the model can access in the power-specific VLP stage is much smaller than that of the general VLP, the representation of the pre-trained VFM inevitably suffers from an overfitting problem.
elements.sent-2411-112: We find this problem is exacerbated when only the supervised learning objective is used during the fine-tuning stage, leading to inferior fine-tuning results, as shown by the dotted lines in Fig.~\ref{fig_2 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-113: Specifically, the labels used in supervised learning provide stronger supervision signals, leading to more discriminative representations and aggravating overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-114: On the other hand, contrastive learning uses natural language to provide weaker supervision signals, the learned representations will be less discriminative~\cite{ref21 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: We verified this quantitatively in our experiments, as can be found in Section~\ref{t-sne .}
elements.sent-2411-116: This inspires us to introduce the ITC task which contains the pre-training objective of contrastive learning into the fine-tuning stage to neutralize the strong discriminative nature of supervised learning for alleviating the overfitting problem, as shown in the right side of Fig.~\ref{fig_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-117: Firstly, to address the problem of the classification dataset not containing alt-texts, we randomly use the category name to fill a general template or match a predefined description to generate the alt-text for each image.

elements.sent-2411-131: The experimental images used in this paper were obtained by a power inspection department through UAV aerial photography.
elements.sent-2411-132: By annotating and cropping the original inspection images, we constructed three datasets for pre-training our power-specific VFM, evaluating the performance of different models in defect classification and defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-133: The TL-it dataset contains 23391 image-text-label triplets covering 24 common categories of normal and defective instances in transmission lines, and some samples from the dataset are shown in Table~\ref{tab2 .}
elements.sent-2411-134: Each image in an image-text-label triplet is obtained by cropping an expert-annotated bounding box from an original transmission line image and each label corresponding to an image is also given by domain experts.
elements.sent-2411-135: In particular, each alt-text in an image-text-label triplet is obtained by filling a general template with the category name corresponding to the label or matching the category name with a predefined description.
elements.sent-2411-136: The TLDC dataset includes 9779 images of 14 types of normal and defective categories in the TL-it dataset.
elements.sent-2411-137: The training set and testing set are divided with a 9:1 ratio.
elements.sent-2411-138: The TLDD dataset includes a total of 1,560 images with 7 defective categories contained in the TL-it dataset as well, in which the the training set and testing set are also divided with a 9:1 ratio.
elements.sent-2411-139: We implement the experiments using the PyTorch framework on a workstation with two NVIDIA RTX3090 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-140: Two types of open-source CN-CLIP models are selected as the baseline, namely CN-CLIP with ResNet50 (RN50) image encoder and CN-CLIP with ViT-B-16 (ViT) image encoder.
elements.sent-2411-141: In the stage of power-specific VLP, we resize the resolution of each input image to $224\times224$ and both models are pre-trained for 30 epochs with the Adam optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-142: For the CN-CLIP with RN50, the learning rate and the batch size are set to $1\times10^{-5}$ and 335 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-143: For the CN-CLIP with ViT, the learning rate and the batch size are set to $1\times10^{-6}$ and 120 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-144: Because CN-CLIP is based on Chinese, all English texts used in experiments related to the proposed model are translated into Chinese.
elements.sent-2411-145: Since there are six types of components in the TL-it dataset with both defective and normal categories, we divide the DNC task into six sub-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-146: Apart from GRC, we denoted other sub-tasks as shielding ring comparison (SRC), shockproof hammer comparison (SHC), bolt comparison (BC), insulator comparison (IC) and foreign-body-like object comparison (FC).
elements.sent-2411-147: In the stage of fine-tuning with the pre-training objective, we replace the backbone of the downstream classification or detection model with the image encoder of the pre-trained VFM and keep the default hyperparameter settings of the original model unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-148: In defect classification, we use the top-1 accuracy (Acc) to evaluate the performance.
elements.sent-2411-149: In defect detection, we adopt mean average precision (mAP) as the main metric.
elements.sent-2411-150: In particular, average precision (AP) is obtained by calculating the area under the P-R curve of a certain category which represents the detection accuracy of that category and mAP is the mean AP of all categories.
elements.sent-2411-151: Here we adopt 0.5 as the intersection over union (IoU) threshold in computing APs and the corresponding mAP.
elements.sent-2411-152: In addition, we use parameter quantities (\#params) to evaluate the model size.

elements.sent-2411-206: This paper proposes a VLP-based power-specific VFM, namely TL-CLIP, which provides a more dedicated training starting point for downstream transmission line defect recognition tasks.
elements.sent-2411-207: The training of TL-CLIP is divided into two stages.
elements.sent-2411-208: The first stage is power-specific VLP, where we pre-train the model with ITC and two novel auxiliary pre-training tasks specially designed for the scene of transmission line inspection.
elements.sent-2411-209: Among them, CTM helps the model better capture the semantics of different types of components and thus avoid confusion for categories with similar semantics.
elements.sent-2411-210: On the other hand, DNC makes the model better cognize the abstract concepts about defect and normality so that the model can be more sensitive to defects in the instances.
elements.sent-2411-211: The second stage fine-tunes the model with the pre-training objective, where we use the proposed FTP strategy to fine-tune the pre-trained VFM instead of the traditional supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-212: By fine-tuning the VFM with both the supervised learning objective of the downstream task and the contrastive learning objective of ITC, the overfitting problem of the pre-trained VFM during the fine-tuning process is effectively alleviated, which leads to a higher recognition accuracy in the downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-213: As large-scale multimodal pre-training is receiving widespread attention, this study provides new ideas for the subsequent development of large-scale pre-trained models unique to the power domain.
elements.sent-2411-214: We will further explore the internal relations hidden in the inspection data to provide domain-specific supervision signals for training power-specific VFMs with higher performance.
elements.sent-2411-215: Meanwhile, we will continue to focus on researching more effective fine-tuning strategies to overcome the overfitting problem caused by insufficient data in the power domain.
elements.sent-2411-216: Moreover, we hope to further expand our research to defect recognition tasks in the generation, transmission, transformation, and distribution of power systems, and train more general power-specific VFMs.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) patrol inspection has emerged as a predominant approach in transmission line monitoring owing to its cost-effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-218: Detecting defects in transmission lines is a critical task during UAV patrol inspection.
elements.sent-2411-219: However, due to imaging distance and shooting angles, UAV patrol images often suffer from insufficient defect-related visual information, which has an adverse effect on detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-220: In this article, we propose a novel method for detecting defects in UAV patrol images, which is based on vision-language pretraining for transmission line (VLP-TL) and a progressive transfer strategy (PTS).
elements.sent-2411-221: Specifically, VLP-TL contains two novel pretraining tasks tailored for the transmission line scenario, aimimg at pretraining an image encoder with abundant knowledge acquired from both visual and linguistic information.
elements.sent-2411-222: Transferring the pretrained image encoder to the defect detector as its backbone can effectively alleviate the insufficient visual information problem.
elements.sent-2411-223: In addition, the PTS further improves transfer performance by progressively bridging the gap between pretraining and downstream defection detection.
elements.sent-2411-224: Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves defect detection accuracy by jointly utilizing multimodal information, overcoming the limitations of insufficient defect-related visual information provided by UAV patrol images.
elements.sent-2411-225: The conventional manual inspection mode no longer meets the growing demands for transmission line inspections.
elements.sent-2411-226: In such context, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) patrol inspection, supported by advances in UAV control technologies, has been widely adopted as a new predominant mode\cite{ref1, ref36, ref37 .}
elements.sent-2411-227: One of the core components in a UAV patrol inspection system is a deep learning-based defect detector, which is used to locate and identify defects captured in the patrol images\cite{ref2, ref3 .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Therefore, improving the detection accuracy of defect detectors is crucial for enhancing the performance of UAV patrol inspection systems and ensuring the stable operation of transmission lines.
elements.sent-2411-229: However, the performance of existing detectors trained with UAV patrol images remains suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-230: Specifically, due to the imaging distance and shooting angles of UAV patrol images, components and defects in the images generally exhibit characteristics such as small size, partial occlusion, and complex backgrounds\cite{ref4, ref5, ref6 .}
elements.sent-2411-231: Consequently, these images fail to provide sufficient defect-related visual information, causing the detector trained on such data cannot acquire essential knowledge for accurate detection.
elements.sent-2411-232: Therefore, an intuitive solution is to jointly utilize multimodal information to supplement the insufficiency of visual information.
elements.sent-2411-233: In the last few years, vision-language pretraining (VLP) has achieved impressive few-shot and even zero-shot recognition performance by learning transferable feature representations from web-scale image-text pairs\cite{ref15, ref16 .}
elements.sent-2411-234: The success of VLP comes from harnessing the complementary nature of the two different modalities (image and text) to provide additional information that enables the model to learn more comprehensive knowledge\cite{ref35,ref53 .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Inspired by this, we propose a transmission line defect detection method from UAV patrol images based on VLP.
elements.sent-2411-236: The overview of the proposed method is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-237: Firstly, we design VLP for transmission lines (VLP-TL) to pretrain an image encoder capable of extracting features tailored for the transmission line scenario by learning from the rich visual and linguistic information contained in transmission line image-text pairs.
elements.sent-2411-238: Through the VLP-TL, the image encoder acquires extensive professional knowledge, thus, the problem of insufficient defect-related visual information provided by UAV patrol images is significantly mitigated.
elements.sent-2411-239: Subsequently, we propose a progressive transfer strategy (PTS) to further improve the performance when transferring the pretrained image encoder to defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-240: The main contributions of this article are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-241: Then, we build a multimodal dataset composed of transmission line image-text pairs to implement VLP-TL.
elements.sent-2411-242: By fully exploiting visual and linguistic information within the dataset, VLP-TL can pretrain an image encoder with abundant knowledge as the backbone of the defect detector.
elements.sent-2411-243: The experimental results show that our detector can effectively improve the defect detection accuracy in UAV patrol images.
elements.sent-2411-246: Finally, Section~\ref{sec5 concludes this work and discusses the future work.}
elements.sent-2411-247: Although the images collected in UAV inspections have high resolution, the components and defects within them often occupy only very small areas, accompanied by partial occlusion and complex backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-248: Such images provide limited defect-related visual information, making it difficult for detectors trained on them to learn sufficient knowledge to support accurate defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-249: To address this problem, this article leverages linguistic information through VLP to supplement the insufficient visual information.
elements.sent-2411-250: Furthermore, a transfer learning strategy is proposed to bridge VLP and defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-251: This section introduces previous works related to this article.
elements.sent-2411-252: VLP is a type of pretraining paradigm that has prevailed in recent years\cite{ref17 .}
elements.sent-2411-253: It uses semantic-rich texts instead of predefined labels to provide supervision signals for pretraining an image encoder with abundant knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-254: The pretrained image encoder can subsequently be transferred as the backbone of downstream task models, achieving amazing performance even with limited training samples\cite{ref29, ref30, ref31 .}
elements.sent-2411-255: According to the application scenarios, existing VLP algorithms can be divided into two types: 1) general-purpose VLP algorithms and 2) domain-specific VLP algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-256: General-purpose VLP algorithms are typically based on pretraining tasks such as image-text contrastive learning and leverage large-scale image-text pairs collected from the internet for pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-257: After pertaining, a wide range of public datasets covering a wide range of downstream tasks are used to evaluate the effect of pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-258: These algorithms strive to achieve consistent improvements across various downstream tasks by scaling up model size\cite{ref46 , improving data quality\cite{ref47 , and refining pretraining tasks\cite{ref21 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-259: However, for application scenarios of special domains, directly transferring image encoders pretrained by general-purpose VLP algorithms does not yield satisfactory results due to the lack of consideration for data privacy and uniqueness.
elements.sent-2411-260: Therefore, another line of works focuses on designing domain-specific VLP algorithms for specialized domains such as remote sensing\cite{ref34 , biomedicine\cite{ref32 , industrial inspection\cite{ref18 , etc. They customize dedicated VLP algorithms according to the characteristics or requirements of a certain domain to learn professional knowledge from domain-specific image-text pairs\cite{ref33 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-261: And these VLP algorithms are evaluated only on a few specified downstream tasks of interest to the domain.
elements.sent-2411-262: Unfortunately, there are no domain-specific VLP algorithms for the domain of electric power, particularly for the transmission line scenario.
elements.sent-2411-263: Therefore, to pretrain an image encoder suitable for processing UAV patrol images in this scenario, we propose the VLP-TL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-264: Specifically, we design two transmission line-specific pretraining tasks based on the implicit relations between samples to efficiently utilize the visual and linguistic information, thereby acquiring richer knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-265: Benefiting from information from both modalities, the pretrained image encoder is then transferred as the backbone of the detector, which can effectively overcome the problem of insufficient defect-related visual information in UAV patrol images and extract more expressive features for defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-266: Transfer learning aims to improve the performance of a downstream model by transferring the knowledge acquired during pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-267: For instance, in defect detection research of transmission lines, a common transfer learning approach is to use an image encoder pretrained on ImageNet\cite{ref43 as the backbone of the defect detector to improve training effectiveness and efficiency\cite{ref12,ref13,ref14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-268: With the advancement of VLP, researchers have realized that simply using a VLP-based image encoder as the backbone for downstream tasks cannot yield optimal transfer performance due to differences in data distribution and training objectives\cite{ref48 .}
elements.sent-2411-269: To better transfer the rich knowledge learned by VLP, VLP-based transfer learning strategies have also become a research hotspot.
elements.sent-2411-270: Based on whether new parameters are introduced beyond the pretrained backbone and task-specific decoder (e.g., neck and head in detectors) in the downstream model, VLP-based transfer learning strategies can be divided into two types: 1) learnable parameter-based and 2) training skill-based.
elements.sent-2411-271: The introduction of these parameters mainly takes the form of prompt vectors\cite{ref29,ref50 and adapter blocks\cite{ref49,ref51 .}}
elements.sent-2411-272: On the other hand, training skill-based strategies do not introduce additional parameters but fine-tune the entire pretrained image encoder or task-specific decoder on downstream datasets with elaborate training skills.
elements.sent-2411-273: For example, Lin et al.\cite{ref53 introduced category names in the language modality as additional training samples to the training of downstream classification tasks, effectively improving few-shot classification performance.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Goyal et al.\cite{ref52 proposed finetuning like you pretrain (FLYP), which replaces the training objective of downstream classification tasks with the pretraining objective of contrastive learning to achieve higher classification accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-275: However, regardless of the type of transfer learning strategy, their targeted downstream tasks are mainly image classification.
elements.sent-2411-276: Unlike detection tasks, classification tasks use instance-level images (where the sample occupies most of the image area) and even not require task-specific decoders.
elements.sent-2411-277: As a result, these existing strategies cannot be directly applied to downstream tasks such as defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-278: To enhance the transfer performance of the pretrained image encoder in defect detection, this article introduces an intermediate transition stage between pretraining and defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-279: By maintaining the pretraining tasks unchanged while further optimizing the image encoder with image styles closer to defect detection, the image encoder is progressively transferred to defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-280: Common transmission line defects involve component damage as well as external interference that poses some hidden dangers.
elements.sent-2411-281: This article focuses on larger-scale defects in transmission lines, such as fitting damage, insulator damage, and foreign bodies.
elements.sent-2411-282: To detect these defects, existing methods improve general-purpose object detectors that perform well on public datasets according to the data distribution or visual characteristics of defects, and then use private defect detection datasets to train dedicated defect detectors\cite{ref6,ref11,ref26 .}
elements.sent-2411-283: Among these previous works, some improvements take the form of introducing more sophisticated network structures to help the detector better capture the professional knowledge hidden in UAV patrol images.
elements.sent-2411-284: For example, Bao et al.\cite{ref22 equipped the defect detector with an end-to-end coordinate attention module, enabling the detector to focus on key components rather than complex backgrounds.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Guo et al.\cite{ref23 designed a new feature enhancement structure to obtain a more robust feature representation for detecting corrosion defects in fittings.}
elements.sent-2411-286: Meanwhile, some improvements aim to help the model better learn professional knowledge by improving essential factors in training, such as training data and training pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-287: For instance, Li et al.\cite{ref25 introduced an intersection‐over‐union‐based sampling strategy to alleviate the problem of class imbalance.}
elements.sent-2411-288: Hao et al.\cite{ref7 adopted a three-step training pipeline to transfer the prior knowledge about insulator parallel-gap faults into a defect detector.}
elements.sent-2411-289: However, these works only utilize the limited defect-related visual information in UAV patrol images to train detectors, thus, the knowledge that detectors can learn is constrained.
elements.sent-2411-290: Realizing this problem, some researchers have turned to other forms of supplementary information.
elements.sent-2411-291: Choi et al.\cite{ref9 presented a multimodal image feature fusion module that introduces visual information provided by infrared images while utilizing conventional visible-light images collected by UAVs.}
elements.sent-2411-292: Falahatnejad et al.\cite{ref63 resorted to a super-resolution-based approach to help the detector access more subtle information in images.}
elements.sent-2411-293: Zhang et al.\cite{ref28 employed self-supervised learning to leverage the visual information contained in a large number of unlabeled normal components, injecting the latent knowledge into the backbone of the detector.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Nevertheless, these methods still cannot completely get rid of the problem of insufficient defect-related visual information.
elements.sent-2411-295: Specifically, whether defects in infrared images and images obtained through super-resolution, or unlabeled normal components in visible-light images, they still exhibit visual characteristics such as small size, partial occlusion, or complex background.
elements.sent-2411-296: In this article, we endeavor to solve this problem by introducing linguistic information.
elements.sent-2411-297: Intuitively, by aligning an image of shockproof hammer intersection with the textual description "two shockproof hammers closely adjacent", the knowledge about this defect can be learned more clearly, even when the shockproof hammers in the image are small or occluded.
elements.sent-2411-298: Through VLP, we successfully utilize linguistic information to train a detector's backbone, which improves defect detection accuracy without compromising the inference speed and detector size.
elements.sent-2411-299: In this section, we introduce our method in detail.
elements.sent-2411-300: It employs a three-stage training pipeline, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_1 : 1) pretraining; 2) transition; and 3) defect detection.}
elements.sent-2411-301: First, a transmission line-specific VLP algorithm, namely VLP-TL, is used to pretrain a dedicated image encoder by leveraging both visual and linguistic information.
elements.sent-2411-302: Second, a transfer learning strategy called PTS is implemented to facilitate a smooth transition between pretraining and downstream defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-303: Finally, the obtained image encoder is transferred to defect detection by serving as the backbone of the defect detector.
elements.sent-2411-304: We propose the VLP-TL algorithm to efficiently utilize transmission line image-text pairs and inject the contained knowledge into the detector's backbone, as shown in Stage 1 of Fig.~\ref{fig_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-305: However, such a description typically only characterizes a small defect region (often covering less than 10\% of the image area).
elements.sent-2411-306: These image-text pairs cannot be directly applied to VLP, as the extensive background areas would severely interfere with the alignment between the two modalities.
elements.sent-2411-307: Therefore, we reorganize the data through three steps to construct a multimodal dataset suitable for VLP.
elements.sent-2411-308: First, we annotate and crop the original UAV patrol images by category to obtain numerous instance-level images of defect and normal samples.
elements.sent-2411-309: Additionally, we reserve the category annotation for each image for the convenience of implementing pretraining tasks.
elements.sent-2411-310: Second, we fill predefined templates with category annotations while manually refining the description provided by inspectors to generate diverse alt-texts for each category, thereby creating an alt-text pool.
elements.sent-2411-311: Finally, we produce image-text pairs by randomly assigning an alt-text from the alt-text pool to each instance-level image according to its category.
elements.sent-2411-312: Through the above process, we construct a multimodal dataset where each image-text pair describes a sample.
elements.sent-2411-313: On this basis, two novel pretraining tasks designed for transmission line samples are introduced to mine the potential knowledge in the multimodal dataset.
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=2.7in]{inspire_srj.pdf} \caption{Relations between different transmission line samples. STSS, STDS, and DT are abbreviations for same type and same status, same type but different status, and different type, respectively. } \label{fig_2}
elements.sent-2411-315: According to the categories of two samples, different samples in public datasets such as ImageNet\cite{ref43 typically exhibit only two relations, i.e., same and different.}
elements.sent-2411-316: In contrast, in the transmission line scenario, there are three probable relations between two samples, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-317: For example, the relation between Sample 1 and Sample 3 is STSS, because both of them are grading rings and exhibit defect status.
elements.sent-2411-318: For example, the relation between Sample 1 and Sample 2 is STDS, because both samples are grading rings, but one is defect while the other is normal.
elements.sent-2411-319: For example, the relation between Sample 1 and Sample 4 is DT, because Sample 1 is a grading ring while Sample 4 is a shielded ring.
elements.sent-2411-320: Specially, for external interference defects (e.g., bird nests and foreign bodies), only STSS and DT relations may exist between them and other samples.
elements.sent-2411-321: Learning to classify the relation between two samples into the above three cases facilitates the discovery of cross-component distinctions while capturing both shared features and subtle variations among samples of the same component type.
elements.sent-2411-322: The knowledge acquired in this way can reduce both misjudgments on component types and false alarms on normal components during defect inspection.
elements.sent-2411-323: Inspired by this, we propose the SRJ pretraining task to learn how to judge the relation between two arbitrary samples, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-324: Given a batch of samples, we denote the images in the batch as $\bm{I =[\bm{i _1,\bm{i _2,\cdots,\bm{i _n]$ and their alt-texts as $\bm{T =[\bm{t _1,\bm{t _2,\cdots,\bm{t _n]$, where $n$ is the batch size.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-325: We feed the images into the image encoder and the alt-texts into the text encoder to extract features from both modalities: \begin{equation}\bm{V}=f_{i}(\bm{I}) \quad and \quad \bm{L}=f_{t}(\bm{T}) \, ,\end{equation} where $f_{i}\left ( \cdot \right )$ and $f_{t}\left ( \cdot \right )$ represent the image encoder and the text encoder, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-326: Then, shuffle feature vectors within $\bm{V}$ and $\bm{L}$ to obtain two shuffled feature matrices: \begin{equation}\bm{V}^{\prime}=\text{Shuffle}(\bm{V}) \quad and \quad \bm{L}^{\prime}=\text{Shuffle}(\bm{L}) \, ,\end{equation} where $\text{Shuffle}\left ( \cdot \right )$ represents the shuffle operation.
elements.sent-2411-327: Next, select one original feature matrix and one shuffled feature matrix to judge the relations between two samples corresponding to feature vectors at the same positions.
elements.sent-2411-328: According to different modality combinations, SRJ can be divided into four parallel subtasks: a) SRJ-IT: implemented with $\bm{V}$ and $\bm{L ^{\prime}$; b) SRJ-TI: implemented with $\bm{L}$ and $\bm{V ^{\prime}$; c) SRJ-II: implemented with $\bm{V}$ and $\bm{V ^{\prime}$; d) SRJ-TT: implemented with $\bm{L}$ and $\bm{L ^{\prime}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-329: Taking SRJ-IT as an example, we concatenate $\bm{V}$ and $\bm{L ^{\prime}$ and use a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) shared by the four subtasks to predict the relations: \begin{equation}\bm{P}_\text{srj-it} = \text{MLP}\left(\text{Concat}\left( \bm{V}, \bm{L^{\prime}} \right) \right),\end{equation} where $\text{MLP}\left ( \cdot \right )$ represents a MLP module, and $\text{Concat}\left ( \cdot \right )$ represents the concatenation operation.}
elements.sent-2411-330: Subsequently, the loss of SRJ-IT can be calculated as: \begin{equation}L_\text{srj-it} = \text{CE}\left(\bm{P}_\text{srj-it}, \bm{Y}_\text{srj-it}\right),\end{equation} where $\text{CE}\left ( \cdot \right )$ represents the cross entropy loss function.
elements.sent-2411-331: Finally, we combine the loss of four subtasks to calculate the loss of SRJ: \begin{equation}L_\text{srj} =\left( L_\text{srj-it} + L_\text{srj-ti} + L_\text{srj-ii} + L_\text{srj-tt} \right) /4 \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-332: We further propose the DNC pretraining task, which requires the image encoder to compare multiple samples with the same component type and determine whether any two samples share identical states, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_4 .}
elements.sent-2411-333: As can be found that each component type is associated with distinct categories.
elements.sent-2411-334: For instance, the "grading ring" component type is associated with categories such as "normal grading ring" and "grading ring damage".
elements.sent-2411-335: Samples with the same component type and from different categories share substantial visual commonalities, while their differences primarily stem from variations in status, background, and viewing angles.
elements.sent-2411-336: As can be seen from the images of Sample 1 to Sample 3 in Fig.~\ref{fig_2 , they all depict a cyclic structure at the end of an insulator string.}
elements.sent-2411-337: Beyond background and viewing angle differences, the key distinction lies in their status: Sample 1 and Sample 3 show detached cyclic structures, while Sample 2 displays a stably fixed structure surrounding the insulator string.
elements.sent-2411-338: Therefore, comparing samples with the same component type reveals discriminative visual cues of different status, which enables more accurate defect detection and reduces false alarms.
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=3.4in]{dnc.pdf} \caption{The implementation of DNC, taking the case of grading rings as an example.} \label{fig_4}
elements.sent-2411-339: For a given batch of samples, we first filter images of defect and normal samples belonging to a specific component type using the reserved category annotations.
elements.sent-2411-340: We denote the images of filtered defect samples as $\bm{I ^\text{d =[\bm{i ^\text{d _1,\bm{i ^\text{d _2,\cdots,\bm{i ^\text{d _k]$ and the images of filtered normal samples as $\bm{I ^\text{n =[\bm{i ^\text{n _1,\bm{i ^\text{n _2,\cdots,\bm{i ^\text{n _q]$, where $k$ and $q$ are the number of defect and normal samples.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-341: Then we feed $\bm{I ^\text{d}$ and $\bm{I ^\text{n}$ into the image encoder to extract features of defect and normal status: \begin{equation}\bm{V}^\text{d}=f_{i}(\bm{I}^\text{d}) \quad and \quad \bm{V}^\text{n}=f_{i}(\bm{I}^\text{n}) \, ,\end{equation} where $\bm{V ^\text{d =[\bm{v ^\text{d _1,\bm{v ^\text{d _2,\cdots,\bm{v ^\text{d _k]$ and $\bm{V ^\text{n =[\bm{v ^\text{n _1,\bm{v ^\text{n _2,\cdots,\bm{v ^\text{n _q]$ represent the image feature matrix of defect and normal samples, and each feature vectors within $\bm{V ^\text{d}$ and $\bm{V ^\text{n}$ corresponding to the images at the same position of $\bm{I ^\text{d}$ and $\bm{I ^\text{n}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-342: Next, we concatenate $\bm{V ^\text{d}$ and $\bm{V ^\text{n}$ to obtain a joint image feature matrix encompassing both defect and normal status: \begin{equation}\bm{V^\text{j}} = \text{Concat}\left( \bm{V}^\text{d}, \bm{V}^\text{n} \right)\, ,\end{equation} where $\bm{V^\text{j }$ is the joint image feature matrix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: After that, we compute the similarity between every two feature vectors within $\bm{V^\text{j }$, which can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\bm{S} = \bm{V^\text{j}}\odot(\bm{V^\text{j}})^{\top}= (s_{ij})_{(k+q)\times(k+q)}\, ,\end{equation} where $\odot$ represents dot product, and $\bm{S}$ is the similarity matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Then, the loss of DNC on the specific component type can be calculated as: \begin{equation}L_\text{dnc}^c = \text{BCE}\left(\bm{S}, \bm{Z}\right)\, ,\end{equation} where $\text{BCE}\left ( \cdot \right )$ represents the binary cross entropy loss function.
elements.sent-2411-345: The total loss of DNC is computed as the weighted sum of losses across all component types: \begin{equation}L_\text{dnc} =\sum_{c=1}^{r}\alpha_c L_\text{dnc}^c \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-346: In addition to SRJ and DNC, we retain image-text contrastive learning (ITC) \cite{ref15,ref16 that used in training the open-source pretrained model as a fundamental pretraining task to avoid the corruption of pretrained representations.}
elements.sent-2411-347: Therefore, the total pretraining loss of VLP-TL is: \begin{equation}\label{totalloss} L_\text{vlp-tl} = \lambda_{1}L_\text{itc} + \lambda_{2}L_\text{srj} + \lambda_{3}L_\text{dnc} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-348: When transferring the pretrained image encoder to defect detection, two latent factors lead to suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-349: To mitigate the gap in training data and training objectives between pertaining and defect detection, PTS introduces an additional transition stage to progressively transfer the pretrained image encoder to defect detection, as shown in Stage 2 of Fig.~\ref{fig_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-350: From the perspective of training data, PTS introduces instance-level images with context for a smoother transition.
elements.sent-2411-351: An instance-level image with context is an image containing both an object of interest and its surrounding environment.
elements.sent-2411-352: For example, the instance-level image with context about the insulator bunch-drop in Fig.~\ref{fig_5 not only contains the vacant position of an insulator but also includes adjacent insulator strings around it.}
elements.sent-2411-353: To obtain instance-level images with context we perform multiscale cropping on the original UAV patrol images from the TLDD dataset, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_5 .}
elements.sent-2411-354: Given a defect region of size $h\times w$ in the UAV patrol image, we define three regions centered on the defect with sizes $3h\times 3w$, $5h\times 5w$, and $7h\times 7w$.
elements.sent-2411-355: Within the three regions, we randomly crop three instance-level images with context whose sizes are $h_1\times w_1$, $h_2\times w_2$, and $h_3\times w_3$.
elements.sent-2411-356: Specifically, $h \leq h_1 \leq 3h$, $3h \leq h_2 \leq 5h$, $5h \leq h_3 \leq 7h$, and $w \leq w_1 \leq 3w$, $3w \leq w_2 \leq 5w$, $5w \leq w_3 \leq 7w$.
elements.sent-2411-357: During the transition stage, we mix the obtained instance-level images with context into the multimodal dataset used in pretraining for training.
elements.sent-2411-358: Since the instance-level images with context better match the style of original UAV patrol images, they can bridge the gap in training data between pretraining and defect detection.
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=3.4in]{imgwcx.pdf} \caption{The method to obtain instance-level images with context.} \label{fig_5}
elements.sent-2411-359: From the perspective of training objectives, PTS retains the ITC pretraining task in the transition stage.
elements.sent-2411-360: The ITC is reused to preserve pretrained representations and alignment between the two modalities, while SRJ and DNC are continually implemented to capture more fine-grained visual cues from instance-level images with context.
elements.sent-2411-361: Thus, the total loss during the transition stage maintains the same formulation as in the pretraining stage (i.e., Eq.\ref{totalloss ).}
elements.sent-2411-362: Through bridging pretraining and defect detection through coordinated data and objective adaptation, PTS can effectively improve defect detection accuracy without introducing additional parameters or compromising inference speed.
elements.sent-2411-363: After pretraining and transition, we transfer the obtained image encoder to defect detection, as shown in Stage 3 of Fig.~\ref{fig_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-364: We construct a specialized detection decoder following the architecture of ViTDet \cite{ref41 on the top of the pretrained image encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-365: Specifically, we first use the image encoder as the backbone of the defect detector and add a simple feature pyramid network (FPN) on the output end of it to provide multiscale features for defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-366: Then we build a region proposal network (RPN) and a box head to process the multiscale features to locate and classify defects that appear in the input images.
elements.sent-2411-367: Finally, the transfer to defect detection is complete after the entire detector is trained on the annotated original UAV patrol images from the TLDD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-368: In this section, we first introduce the datasets used in our experiments and the experimental settings.
elements.sent-2411-369: Then, we evaluate the proposed method by comparing it with other existing methods and implementing ablation experiments.
elements.sent-2411-370: The experimental images used in this article are collected by UAVs equipped with high-definition image transmission systems during patrol inspections.
elements.sent-2411-371: We manually filter and remove the blurred images to ensure data quality.
elements.sent-2411-372: Afterward, we select a part of defect-containing images and annotate the defects within them to build the TLDD dataset for training and testing the detectors.
elements.sent-2411-373: For the remaining images, we annotate and crop both normal and defect components and external interference defects to build the multimodal dataset for pertaining.
elements.sent-2411-374: The multimodal dataset is a pertaining dataset curated for the proposed VLP-TL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-375: It is designed to pretrain the image encoder with image-text pairs containing rich multimodal information.
elements.sent-2411-376: The multimodal dataset contains 23,391 samples in the form of image-text pairs and covers 24 common normal and defect categories about adjusting boards, composite insulators, disc suspension insulators, glass insulators, grading rings, yoke plates, suspension clamps, shielded rings, shockproof hammers, weight, bird nests, foreign bodies, etc. It's noteworthy that we also reserve the category annotation for each image-text pair so that we can perform SRJ and DNC pretraining tasks efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-377: Some typical image-text pairs and the corresponding category annotations in the multimodal dataset are shown in Table \ref{tab1 .}
elements.figure-2411-023: \caption{Some Samples in the Multimodal Dataset for Pretraining} \label{tab1} \begin{tabular}{m{2.4cm}<{ }m{2.6cm}<{ }m{2.5cm}<{ }m{2.4cm}<{ }} Instance-level Images Alt-texts Category Annotations \includegraphics[width=0.8in]{tab1_1.png} There is a normal grading ring in the image. normal grading ring \includegraphics[width=0.8in]{tab1_2.png} A rusted shielded ring on the aerial inspection image. shielded ring corrosion \includegraphics[width=0.8in]{tab1_3.png} Two shockproof hammers are closely adjacent to each other. shockproof hammer intersection \end{tabular}

(c) A defect of shielded ring corrosion. (d) A defect of shockproof hammer intersection. (e) A defect of foreign body. (e) A defect of bird nest and a defect of insulator bunch-drop.} \label{fig_6}
elements.sent-2411-378: The TLDD dataset is a specialized dataset for transmission line defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-379: We use the TLDD dataset to train different types of detectors and evaluate their performance on defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-380: In our method, the TLDD dataset is used to provide instance-level images with context for transition and transfer the pretrained image encoder to the defect detection task.
elements.sent-2411-381: The TLDD dataset includes a total of 1,830 images with 7 defect categories: 1) bird nest (BN); 2) foreign body (FB); 3) grading ring damage (GRD); 4) insulator bunch-drop (IBD); 5) insulator damage (ID); 6) shielded ring corrosion (SRC); 7) shockproof hammer intersection (SHI).
elements.sent-2411-382: Some samples in the TLDD dataset and their annotations are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_6 .}
elements.sent-2411-383: Before pretraining, we use the open-source pretrained weights of CN-CLIP ViT-B-16 \cite{ref39 to initialize the image encoder and text encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-384: In the stage of pretraining, we resize the resolution of each input image to $224\times224$.
elements.sent-2411-385: We pretrain both encoders for 30 epochs with the Adam optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-386: The initial learning rate is set to $1\times10^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-387: The batch size is adjusted to 120 according to the available memory of the used devices.
elements.sent-2411-388: In the stage of transition, we continue to optimize the pretrained image encoder and text encoder for 15 epochs while keeping other hyperparameters unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-389: In the stage of defect detection, we adopt the multiscale training strategy to adapt the image encoder to defect detection, requiring higher input resolution.
elements.sent-2411-390: We train the defect detector for 50 epochs with the AdamW optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-391: The initial learning rate is $1\times10^{-5}$ for the backbone and $1\times10^{-4}$ for other modules in the detection decoder.
elements.sent-2411-392: To evaluate the detection accuracy, we adopt mean average precision (mAP) as the main metric.
elements.sent-2411-393: According to different intersection over union (IoU) thresholds used in calculating AP, mAP can be divided into $\text{mAP _{50}$, $\text{mAP _{75}$, and $\text{mAP _{50:95}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-394: Among them, $\text{mAP _{50}$ and $\text{mAP _{75}$ represents the mAP values calculated under IoU thresholds of 0.5 and 0.75, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-395: Additionally, we use the number of parameters (Params) to evaluate the detector size and frame per second (FPS) to evaluate the inference speed of the detector.

elements.sent-2411-409: In order to verify the effectiveness of the proposed VLP-TL and PTS, we design a set of ablation experiments for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-410: In particular, we further analyze some important factors involved in VLP-TL and PTS.
elements.sent-2411-411: When neither VLP-TL nor PTS is utilized, the baseline detector is a ViTDet whose backbone is initialized with the pretrained weights of CN-CLIP.
elements.sent-2411-412: Based on the baseline detector, we introduce the VLP-TL and PTS separately to investigate the effect of each improvement scheme.
elements.sent-2411-413: When VLP-TL is employed to pretrain the backbone of the detector, $\text{mAP _{50}$ of the detector is improved by 2.3\% while the corresponding APs of all categories are improved to varying degrees.}
elements.sent-2411-414: This verifies that VLP-TL can effectively mine the domain knowledge contained in the multimodal dataset, thereby making the backbone more suitable for the transmission line scenario.
elements.sent-2411-415: On the other hand, when only PTS is used to transfer the pretrained image encoder to defect detection, $\text{mAP _{50}$ is improved by 1.2\%.}
elements.sent-2411-416: But overall, when only using PTS, the increases in $\text{mAP _{50}$ and AP of each category are not as good as those of VLP-TL.}
elements.sent-2411-417: This is because there is a lack of sufficient exposure to instance-level images in the pretraining stage.
elements.sent-2411-418: Specifically, directly introducing instance-level images with context increases the difficulty of aligning the two modalities, thereby hindering the image encoder from learning professional knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-419: Additionally, it can be seen that neither improvement scheme introduces additional parameters, because they are improved from the perspective of training strategy without modifying the original detector architecture.
elements.sent-2411-420: Our method is improved by combining the two improvement schemes, resulting in a 3.9\% increase in $\text{mAP _{50}$ and significant increases in AP across all categories.}

elements.sent-2411-422: Analysis of pretraining data: To evaluate the impact of multimodal information in the pretraining data on defect detection, we compare the defect detection performance of VLP that jointly uses vision and language modalities with traditional supervised pretraining using only the vision modality.
elements.sent-2411-423: Concretely, we first build a classification dataset with all images and category annotations from the multimodal dataset.
elements.sent-2411-424: Then, we use the multimodal dataset and the classification dataset to pretraining two image encoders with image classification and ITC, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-425: Finally, we build two defect detectors based on the image encoders and test their detection accuracy, as shown in Table~\ref{tab4 .}
elements.sent-2411-426: It can be found that when we introduce the language modality beyond the vision modality, the $\text{mAP _{50}$, $\text{mAP _{75}$, and $\text{mAP _{50:95}$ are improved by 3.5\%, 1.7\%, and 2.0\%, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-427: It proves that the detector benefits from the additional information introduced by the language modality, so the detection accuracy is effectively improved.

elements.sent-2411-428: Analysis of pretraining tasks: To examine the effect of various pretraining tasks on defect detection, we performed an ablation analysis of the pretraining tasks within the VLP-TL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-429: The results are shown in Table~\ref{tab5 .}
elements.sent-2411-430: It is easy to find that when ITC is not used, the detection accuracy is significantly lower than the case without pretraining, whether using SRJ or DNC.
elements.sent-2411-431: This demonstrates that if ITC is not used as the fundamental pretraining task, the original pretraining representations will be corrupted by other pretraining tasks, which will have an adverse impact on defect detection.
elements.sent-2411-432: On the contrary, when DNC or SRJ tasks are introduced on the basis of ITC, the $\text{mAP _{50}$, $\text{mAP _{75}$, and $\text{mAP _{50:95}$ can be improved to varying degrees.}}}
elements.sent-2411-433: This verifies the effectiveness of the two proposed pretraining tasks.
elements.sent-2411-434: In other words, both SRJ and DNC can further explore the power-related professional knowledge contained in the multimodal pretraining data based on the alignment of transmission line images and alt-texts through ITC.
elements.sent-2411-435: Furthermore, the combination of three pretraining tasks result in 2.3\%, 5.5\%, and 3.6\% increase in $\text{mAP _{50}$, $\text{mAP _{75}$, and $\text{mAP _{50:95}$, which verifying the effectiveness of the VLP-TL algorithm.}}}
elements.sent-2411-436: To intuitively observe the effect of pretraining the image encoder using the proposed VLP-TL algorithm, we select some typical images from the multimodal dataset and visualize the attention maps generated by different image encoders in Fig.~\ref{fig_8 .}
elements.sent-2411-437: Specifically, we compare the attention maps generated by three types of ViT image encoders: ViT pretrained on ImageNet, original ViT in CN-CLIP, and ViT in CN-CLIP pretrained with VLP-TL.
elements.sent-2411-438: The first and second rows of Fig.~\ref{fig_8 are normal and damaged grading rings, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-439: As can be seen from the first row, the image encoder pretrained with VLP-TL can more accurately focus on the ring-shaped area corresponding to the grading ring.
elements.sent-2411-440: More impressively, the image encoder pretrained with VLP-TL accurately locates the damaged grading ring and two breakpoints in the second row, while two other image encoders fail.
elements.sent-2411-441: The third and fourth rows of Fig.~\ref{fig_8 show normal shockproof hammers and a shockproof hammer intersection defect, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-442: In the third row, the image encoder pretrained with VLP-TL can find the shockproof hammer while also noting the two different cables, thereby avoiding the misjudgment of the intersection defect.
elements.sent-2411-443: In the fourth row, the image encoder pretrained with VLP-TL correctly finds the two shockproof hammers and their intersection point.
elements.sent-2411-444: However, the two other image encoders both fail to locate the shockproof hammers in the third and fourth rows.
elements.sent-2411-445: In summary, through pretraining with VLP-TL, the image encoder is more sensitive to components and defects in transmission lines, indicating the benefit of multimodal information for the image encoder.
